g.data-narration: Welcome to Android Ecosystem - your complete guide to understanding Android OS fundamentals.
The Android ecosystem is a complete mobile platform with hardware, software, and services working together.
Android was created by the Open Handset Alliance - a consortium of leading tech companies.
Android versions are released periodically, introducing new features, improvements, and security patches.
API levels define the programming interface, ensuring backward compatibility across Android versions.
An Android Activity represents a single screen with user interface - the building block of Android apps.
Activities have lifecycle states: Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, and Destroyed.
Android provides powerful features like multi-touch support and access to device sensors for rich app experiences.
Android supports background execution and device administration for enterprise and power-user scenarios.
The Android architecture stack has five main components: Application, Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, and ContentProvider.
Android is built on the Linux kernel, providing security, performance optimization, and customization capabilities.
The complete Android architecture stack showing all layers from Applications down to the Linux Kernel.

g.text: Application Framework (Activity Manager, Window Manager, etc.)
Native Libraries (SQLite, WebKit, OpenGL, Media Framework)
Linux Kernel (Device drivers, Memory management, Process management)

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Android ecosystem combines hardware, software, services. Activities are UI screens with lifecycle. Linux kernel provides security and performance.

g.text: Android Ecosystem = Hardware + Software + Google Services
Activities = Single UI screens with lifecycle states
